coaching.title: Today's Run
coaching.message: Sunday in peak phase: this is your second long effort of the week, so dial it back to Z2 steady-state for 90–110 minutes on rolling or slightly technical terrain. You're 110 days out and your aerobic system is sharp; use today to log volume and practice footwork on uneven ground without accumulating central fatigue on top of yesterday's effort. Target HR 135–150 bpm depending on terrain. If legs feel heavy, 75 minutes easy is acceptable—never chase a number when your CNS is already loaded from the week's quality work.

coaching.title: Fuel Right
coaching.message: You're in peak training load; your glycogen stores are a limiting factor. Eat 1.5–2.0g carbs per kg bodyweight today distributed across all meals—aim for 300–400g total if you weigh ~75kg. Pre-run (2–3 hours prior): oatmeal, banana, honey. On the run: 30–40g carbs per hour via gels or sports drink if you're running >90 minutes. Post-run within 30 minutes: carbs + 20–30g protein to start recovery. Don't skimp on Sunday; it funds Monday's recovery and Tuesday's quality.

coaching.title: Recovery
coaching.message: Prioritize sleep tonight—you're in peak phase and your body is in a state of controlled overreach. Target 8–9 hours minimum. After the run, get 10–15 minutes of easy walking or foam rolling to flush lactate, then hydrate aggressively (500–750ml fluid + electrolytes over 2 hours). If travel is on the agenda tomorrow, pack a foam roller and commit to a 20-minute mobility session before bed. Your parasympathetic nervous system needs as much downtime as your muscles do.

coaching.title: Mental Edge
coaching.message: You're 110 days from an alpine 50K with serious vert and sparse aid—this is the phase where mental toughness gets built in the aerobic zone, not in the pain cave. Today's run is about rhythmic breathing, terrain awareness, and self-regulation: can you stay composed at Z2 when your ego wants to push harder? Practice your race-day mindset: break the Julian Alps into 5–6 segments, focus on the next aid station, not the finish line. That executive discipline translates directly to mountain running.
